speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: (Reading History of Pioneers of 1870-1889-1894 written by Agnes Healy Jones.) The first white girl was divided in two counties and the second county was named Letaw. She was the first white girl born north of the Clearwater River. Her parents came to the area in early 70s and were the first white people to settle north of the river at Thorn Creek. The Indian cattle and horses were plentiful and were very vicious. Mr. Tierney built a small house in Hideout. The Indians did not like to have the white people coming in. The wild game was plentiful and was mainly prairie chickens coming in. They would set a trap, made a large box with a figure four underneath and placed feed there and so many would come in and the box would drop down. After a year, Mr. Tierney built a sizable home of logs with an upstairs adit. They made their cedar shakes by hand and cut logs to build a house. Mr. and Mrs. Tierney always had a welcome for all. They had a post office not authorized for four years to help their new neighbors receive their mail, which was delivered by a man through Palouse, Washington on a pony carried by a saddlebag. J .C. Hanson came in 1972. Michael Evitts arrived in 1870 and it was not long before Jacob Cambridge and the Leeches, Sharnhars, Watcher and Stravich came to the neighborhood in 1874. The Tierney home became a stopover place for people traveling from Spokane and Palouse, Washington to Lewiston, Idaho. Mr. and Mrs. Tierney always welcomed them heartily. They provided the first post office in that reason to come with their neighbors for four years, 1870 -74. Mrs. Tierney was a courageous woman as all other pioneer women. She could ride a horse as fast as any Indian Cayuse could run. She also rode a horseback to Lewiston and carried her baby on a pillow. Good weather they would forge the Clearwater River during the year of 1972 and just before John Silk had built a ferry to cross during the winter in 1874 sadness came to the family. Mrs. Tierney passed away, leaving to mourn her husband, three sons and a daughter. It was a bad winter and the snow was deep. The remains were taken to Moscow for burial near the little chapel on the hill in the Moscow cemetery. After Mrs. Tierney passed, all Mrs. Dreyfus took care of Annie and then she was placed with different people. She was with Mr. and Mrs. Jim Kearney in Lewiston at that time and the Indians were quite uncivilized. They used to fly fish along the rivers. The Lewis and Clark Hotel now is located. There were many horror stories regarding the Indian territory in those days during June 1877. Mrs. Kearney woke Annie up and told her to get up quick. The Indians were coming. Annie was so frightened she claimed she could feel her hairs raise on her head. All women and children were hastened to a church on Main Street by men on horses and any other means of transportation. A Mr. Martin Foster, a friend of the family, picked Annie up and kissed her goodbye and said, I will not be back. He climbed on his pony and rode away. He was shot and they also shot his horse. Both are buried there and the marker is still there. This battle was on the uprise of the Indians in 1877. The Indians were hostile over white people occupying their land and taking away their freedom. This was a bad battle but they are white settlers. One after the loss of quite a number of white men. The Indians then headed toward Montana and away from Lewiston. Annie never had first, when her parents first settled in Thorn Creek, the Indians were quite bold. Mrs. Turner gave them food and got along with them. The first settlers would go by team and wagon as far as Palouse, Washington to get their provisions and were often overtaken with bad weather and snowstorms. They would sleep under the wagon. Dried apples, coffee and other items came in barrels. Sugar and many items came in boxes and barrels. The sugar was brown and many items in color and the syrup would come to in two gallon jackets. In 1875 the first school, a log house, was built. The teacher, Mr. Monroe, lived at the Tierney home and helped with the children. Annie, at the age of 11, went by team, a little cart, to the De Smet Mission, a school for girls. She would return home at the age of 16 and marry Dan Haley in Thorn Creek in 1887. Mr. Haley had a homestead land west of what is now Genesee, Idaho. She endured many hardships and learned to take care of stock and remain there at times five days alone. While he went to the mountains to bring home the wood supply for winter. She drove horses well and one time when driving two coasts, it's still heck on the way home from town. The coast did all but run away. At that time there were no fences between the town and farms. She had a wild ride over the hills with baby Agnes and her lap. Mr. and Mrs. Haley remained on the farm seven years. They rented the farm and moved to Genesee. The grandson of the home known at that time as his own house in Genesee. In 1893 and 94, Genesee was the most thriving town with population of 1 ,500. The railroad was just new. There was no rail service at Lewiston or up in the Camas Prairie region. This brought all business to Genesee. The stagecoach driven by Felix Warren or Mr. Meeker used the hotel as a staffing place. At times it was raining and cold and the wheel hubs were deep in mud. The passion would climb out weary and tired. The fall season was so wet farmers never completed harvesting their crops. Mr. Haley moved back to the farm and got his usual round of hogs and turned them into green stacks, growing wheat sacks. Saved him financially, lived on the farm a few more years and moved back to town. All pioneer women the same as my mother had no patterns to make clothing. They cut out by chart with stiff paper with lines and dots for marks showing sizes to mark through the cloth. They used tracing wheels to mark lines to cut the cloth. They had to style the dresses according to their own ideas. My mother made all her clothes and baby clothes by hand for four years and then her father presented her with a new home sewing machine. She also knit a lot of lace for trimming, knit all socks for the family and crocheted and knit lace trimming for the children's clothing. She made butter and had the name of being a fine butter maker. An attorney was a religious person. Her mother was born in Denmark and belonged to the Danish Lutheran Church. Her father was born in Åland. Mr. and Mrs. Diana Haley were the parents of four daughters, Agnes, Margot, Lauren, Ethel. Mr. Haley passed away in April 1933 and Mrs. Haley passed March 1953.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Now that's not modern now. Well, of course, Dad. In the early 70s, Dan Haley worked for some time for John, the first settlers, Michael Evitts was with his half brother. John Cambridge came to Thorn Creek area, settled there and Mr. H .B. Jahadjian of Genesee. These are the people Dan Haley worked for. Later years, Mr. Haley took up a homestead three miles from the Evitts farm. He would walk to and from his place's work as it was law to sleep nights in your homestead to prove your rights. This was in 1979. When Mr. Haley was employed at the Jahadjian family, living as far as now Genesee, Idaho, he and the boys would go to the mountains and cut long rails, 10 -12 feet in length and build zigzag fences to keep the hogs in. He took many rails, as they called them, they would spend days at a time. They also cut logs to build farm buildings, small buildings on Mr. Haley's place. They also cut out shakes by hand to cover the roof of buildings. Logs were put in place with wooden pegs. The bears got in Mr. Haley's camp one night and stole all the flower bacon and other provisions. The Hodges boys came to his rescue and gave him aid. People used to go to the mountains and cut them under plywood and haul many courts. The land was all prairie and wildflowers. Mr. Haley plowed his first 20 acres, hand -sewed his first crop by flax, cradled it, then frilled it by hand. They had no string to tie the bundles and used stems of grain to tie it with. He hauled his first crops to Lewiston by team and they'd be hauled down through the river by barge. Like all the other pioneers, he had to cross the ferry up right by John Silkett. It cost 25 cents to cross the river by ferry. Oftentimes the clear water was very low in the summer season. They called Forge River. They could forge the river with light wagon and by horseback. At this time Mr. Haley would go over to the prairie and buy stock hogs, driven by them all the way up the Lewiston hill by the horseback and the help of a hard man. Mr. Haley spent all his life around Genesee. He had the first milk wagon in 1887. The town began to grow in heaps and leaps. Beginning in 1877, the first merchants were Rosenstein and Levi's stores. Hodges and Cox were the druggists. This is the late layer known as Old Town. Home was beginning to be built in this area and the railroad survey came in 1886. It stopped half a mile on this side. All the residents began to move houses. Rosenstein moved his store building and house to the present site now occupied by the medical building. It took three months to winch it alone. Mr. Rosenstein had a general store and everything. Merchandise would remain open all day Sunday. Hitching posts in all the streets would be lined with teams. In the summertime Mr. Rosenstein had a special stop made near the store building for the ladies to stand upon it in the easier step. The side saddles. It was in the early days that ladies would not ride any other way on the side saddle. They were long full skirts. First, cobs planted in this area were mostly flax. The ground was rich and yielded large cobs. In the spring the fields were beautiful and blossoms of blue color. In 1879, Mr. John Sullivan came to the neighborhood. Dan Haley wrote to him and stated there was plenty of choice. Land begotten. Joining the homestead, Mr. Haley Sullivan homesteaded 160 acres and 80 acres timber claim. Later he bought 80 acres more. His first fence was three foot deep and three feet wide to keep cattle out of his large potato patch. He drove to the mounds with a team of horses. And the same year that pioneers got the logs and woods, he was driving one day home and it was very cold. He got off of the wagon to walk, get along by walking. He dropped a rein, went to pick it up and the wagon wheels ran over his two forefingers. And out so severely they could not be saved. This did not hinder his plans. He continued to be saved. Put up buildings and lived there until 1908 when he moved to Genesee. He passed away May the 24th. Mrs. Sullivan passed away June the 24th. And in the meantime, at the time Mr. Sullivan came here, my mother and father were living on the ranch in a mountain of leather cobs and had big plans made for the future. And he fell from a haystack and fractured his leg. And that had to be removed so shattered they could not save them days. And it did not discourage him. He wore a peg leg and he rode his buckskin saddle pony all over and rounded up cattle. And he had some cattle out here that he bought from Eli Hickman. And Hickman had them up in a field here owned by a Mrs. Dick Tharton. So Dad went up to get the two stairs and she pulled a revolver on so they could sure if he could claim you cattle. And he got his cattle then later. I read that but my mother didn't. Yes, you did. Well now here's about Grandpa. Oh there's a lot of things I think of you know I could write down but I just. Now, here's one pretty long. When Tom Tierney came to Thorn Creek District there was nothing but boiled flowers and fine bunch grass and lots of thorn bushes along the creek. So they named the district Thorn Creek. Tom Tierney built his first hideout, small cabin of logs and mud and put together wooden pegs. That's still standing. It's a hundred and three years old this fall. 1973. The roof was made of handmade sear shakes. They stored their belongings and provisions. They used tripod to hold them from iron kettles over an out floor fire to do their cooking. They roast the potatoes in the farm built outside. He built a second log cabin consisting of a large room and one room upstairs. In 71, the kitchen had a pantry and a washroom at one end and a little bedroom at the other end. Inside these rooms lined with rustic lumber. This considered a nice home for homesteader. The Tierney cabin was a stopover place for many people who were on their way from Palouse to Washington, Lewis and Idaho. The Pony Express Rider brought mail and saddle bags. The Tierney place was known as Thorn Creek, Idaho territory and for years the new settlers came for mail. After Mrs. Tierney passed away in 74, the mail was brought to the Beeman place, presently known as Nordby's home near Genesee. And the rest of the mail taken to Moscow, Paradise Valley. The Tom Tierney's had three sons and one daughter when the mother passed away leaving the younger of the seven sons seven days old. Snow was very deep and it was cold weather. The neighbors were very kind, taking care of the children. The daughter was taken care of by different families until 11 years of age. Then she was taken to the December submission, north of the present town. She was four and a half years old when she came home. It was four and a half years before she came home. Tom Tierney decided to build a new home and finished lumber in 86. Mr. John S. Sullivan came in 83 and proved on land, on the attorney land. Sullivan built a fine basement to be in the list of new Tierney homes for the price of a cow. Sullivan trade in Ireland was a stone mason. His family arrived in 86 after Tierney was settled and proved on 160 acres timber claim right. He had to plant 10 acres of trees and later on he planted, accumulated more land. The valley was very productive with camas. When the Indians were ready to gather camas, they would camp in the timber culture, near the timber culture. When they were ready to gather, the Indians would arrive, camp there and pick all the camas. They would roast them on heated rock in the timber culture. This timber culture was later owned by a son, Town Jr. In the early 20s he built a small house near the culture. A second to spend the part of the time in the summer months, he later sold it to some insurance company. All buildings made by Tierney were placed where his present buildings are. He always said the finest stall was for a garden. He was east of the house, below where the present barn is now. The first cabinets built of logs. He planted the first fruit trees, later were the life of the children. In 1870 Michael Evitts arrived. He did carpenter work and other work. He always had a kind helping hand for someone. His brother come later, Mr. Evitts filed for his land claims two years later. His wife, a faithful companion, at his side, all time. She could see a field of 160 acres, so neighbors say, that by hand from the back of a wagon, according to Idaho history, he purchased parcels of land different times until he accumulated a large acreage. And then, um,

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Mr. Grandfather Turner rode a horse from Lewiston to ... area in Moscow to look the country over. He talked to Mr. Buchanan, one of the first to settle in Moscow, before he made his choice of place to settle. He said ... had two to three feet of clear water, and at that time, with lots of thorn bushes along the sides. There were many wild bears and wild fars of all kinds. The creek was so clean and clear, then he thought it was most attractive land for miles around. So they settled there, took up 160 acres and 80 acre timber plain. Later he planted trees and cultivated it for the crumbs of those days. You had to be a citizen before you could get title to any land. The government used round rocks for markers. You could plow that, that made a quarter, 40 acres. You would plow to it, and then it was yours to pile on. At that time there were, wasn't a town, a union town, just lots of grass and sunflowers, and few Indian teepees, cattle and horses scattered. The first cabin built of muddle logs was wooden pegs. It was built above the creek. It was built there, where the present house now stands. My grandfather built the second one near this one. The old houses are no longer standing. Before it was built, they would hide out from the Indians. There was lots of good water below the house. Later a new home was built.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Some Indian cattle would get man at the least thing and were not safe to be around at that time.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I'm going to sit there, all right. The Camas route was, copied from the book for the first white woman over the rockets by Drury. The Camas route was an edible tuber, somewhat like an onion. This was one of the main items of food for the Nesburses and the other Indians of that area. They grew here in abundance. It is the principal resort of Cayuses and many other tribes to obtain of it. They were very fond. It resembles an onion in shape and color. When cooked, it is very sweet, tastes like fig. Their manner of baking them is very curious. They dig a hole in the ground, throwing the heap of stones, heat that to red hot, cover with green grass upon which they put Camas, and cover the whole grain with earth, then taken out and it is black. This is the chief food of many tribes during the winter. When the Camas was dried, why they grinded up, mash it up and then make their bread out of it too. In the early days, anyone would do all the driving while their men sit on the wagon beside them. They often got off the wagon, had their babies by themselves off the trail. When my grandfather Thomas Tierney first settled, started farming, the sod had to be broken up before it could be seeded. And this was not an easy task. The walking plow, or foot burner they call it sometimes, was first used to break the sod. The small children could hardly reach the top of the handles. The first crop was cultivated by homemade harrow, made with 4x4s. The ground was rolled by a big log, packed down the soil. They would sow the wheat. They cradled the first crop. It was four o 'clock before they would get to Lewiston to barge it out.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: The first thing they raised was a vegetable garden. My grandfather was first to break up the ground. He liked garden produce and had a fine yield. The first crop produced in the summer of 1871. He raised flax one year, wheat the next. The flax fields were a blue color when in bloom. They were very beautiful. Finally he gave up raising flax and the sod was too rich to grow them. The stems were used to tie them together. The men hewed all the rails cut down for fences. They held them from Cornwall, Idaho. The fences were called Wharram Fences. Her school was built on logs in 1875. It sat on the Joe Brunte place, formed now by Lewis Herman. Later, moved within a quarter of a mile from the Tierneys in Thorn Creek. My mother's name was Annie Tierney. She was the first white baby born in south of Moscow in October 1871. She was baptized by Reverend Father Cataldo in her home at Thorn Creek. She was a very pretty woman with a small figure and nineteen waists. Mike Leavis was her godfather. In 1873, Uncle Will was born. Grandmother Tierney died in January of 1974 and was buried at Moscow. At that time there was deep snow and the horses would be belly deep in the snow most of the way to Moscow. The rocks would be heated and wrapped up. We put in straw for heat. It would take three or four hours to travel to Moscow in bad weather. The older men all wore thick felt boots under their high boot shoes and got real cold in the winter. When it got real cold in the winter, my grandmother's death, after my grandmother's death, Monroe stayed as a grandfather and helped to raise the children. He was a teacher. Grandfather kept my grandmother's saddle pony a long time after she died. Nowadays, a young woman, a young man, often gives his best girlfriend a gift of candy, flowers, or even a carver. But in the early times, the young men would sometimes give their girl a saddle pony. I remember one time my mother told me a young man gave his girlfriend a gray pony for a gift. Of course, I know who the people were. They're all passed away now. Then he heard she was dating another fella, so he slipped into the barn and recovered the pony out the back door. The second cabin that my grandfather built was also made of mud and loaves, shakes for the roof and put together with scurry nails used at that time. Inside was lined with rustic lumber. The shakes for the roof were made by a draw knife. The first cabin on Grand Folks had a pot -bellied cooking container over a tripod. My grandmother would roast the spuds in an open fire. They boiled all the food in the pot and liked everything cooked that way. They never liked fried foods. My grandfather would go to town police once a year for supplies. The families would buy barrels of dried apples, sugar, and few gallons of jackets of syrup. They also bought coffee beans to be roasted. They were green and ground for their use. Spuds and barrels of sauerkraut also were kept in the cellar. The women used to make all their soap out of lye from wood ashes. The prairie chickens were very plentiful in early days. The Indians would cover them with a mud batter, cover and cook over a bonfire. When they would be done, they would pull them out of the fire, strip off the feathers, mud, and all they need to rest. My grandfather had finest horses. I remember two white horses he called Megan and Lucy. He raised horses and traded with the Indians. He also was a great horseman and rode with my grandmother many times. She used the side of the saddle was the custom at that time. The Clearwater River was low and they could forage with their horses. The mail delivered to Thorn Creek in those days. It was carried by horses, saddle bags, and the plu -sway of the Tierney Farm. The post horse was established at the Tierney Farm in the early times. My grandfather turned him out to ride in a phone to Pony Express for six months in the Colorado's near Pikes Peak before he came to Idaho. He also freighted with ox teams for the government during the Civil War. There used to be cuckoo and curlew birds, but you don't see them anymore. They would lay their eggs out in squirrel holes. They were cute and about the shape of barn owl. They would cuckoo in the mornings and just plain. There were also lots of squirrels. The children made pets out of lots of the young things, even chicken hawks. I had a buckskin horse that I would see how fast I could ride her up the hill. In 1901 my grand folks moved to Moscow and lived there first. The brick house was built there. It is okay for the Safeway Store parking lot is situated now. I was the oldest of 28 grandchildren. My grandfather had good health up to the last. He walked down to his garden a few days before he died. He lived to be 94 years of age and passed away August 17, 1924. This is about dead I guess.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, I guess I could put that too. My father, Dan Haley, came to San Francisco from Ireland and around the horn in 1870. He never missed coming to Genesee for St. Patrick's Day, even once in 50 years. He was small of build,

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: but did lots of hard work all his life. He was wise to choosing a land for homestead. My mother always used clean, dry grass hay for sticks used on the beds. We had flowers in our house and mother always used to keep them clean, scrubbed white. The rooms in our home are lined with cedar. My folks had causas and lots of nice work horses and lots of hogs. They used self -feeders for the hogs and dug oils for the winter water supply. They would haul wheat to town for ground, flower, and the bran and shorts, bring back for hog feed when the mills were built close to town. My folks used to cook potatoes for their hogs and they had lots of spuds that year. They put the spuds in the vet with wheat over them to cook them in this way. I was few years old when my folks moved to Genesee. There was lots of saloons, stores, livery stables, and there was lots of time. My father, Dan Haley, were on the Grand Central Hotel in Genesee and charged $5 .50 a week for boarding a room at that time. I remember the drummers coming in with their samples. They always kept a sample room where they would show their merchandise. They would go around to the country and also show the homes and they would take orders and send them back by freight or wherever they could. There were two Chinese launders in Genesee at that time. St. Joseph's schools also starred in Genesee for the Catholic children. The first school was large, built in 1875, and set on the front of the place, formed by Lewis Herman. Later it moved within the Corps of Moi of Ternes and also close to, for other settlers, Turn Creek.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Genesee Valley, seven miles west of Genesee, no, east of Genesee, was called Cow Creek in those days. J .P. Craves and his son, Clyde, built the first electric railroad into Moscow. And after the big train wreck in 1909 and 1910, while they sold out to the great north, and now sold freight from Hauling now, the railroad first came to Genesee in 1888. The old town was built on the east end. Enormous prices were paid for the land for the railroad. So they went some of the buildings over to the side. At the time the railroad was built. When the railroad was built in Lewiston in 1898, it marked the beginning and the end of much shipping and livestock and grains from Genesee. The stagecoach came from Lewiston to Genesee on the Moscow and then up to Cluse. Azar Meeker was one of the coach, stagecoach drivers at that time. I remember seeing stagecoach go by. Once they stopped, I remember some lady had a birdcage, and the bird inside, it was quite fascinating, and with it at the time, I never forgot it. Felix Warren ran the stagecoach of Genesee in 1893 during that time when father ran the Grand Central Hotel. Mr. Warren was a good fellow with a whip and handling his team of horses. It was often said he could flick a flea off a dog's tail, his sister, Mrs. Mary Spalding. His sister was Mrs. Mary Spalding. Stagecoaches changed horses to Smith's Place north of town. Felix Warren wore a long raincoat that would shed water and a large hat. Lots of times there was rain and deep mud to drive through. Felix Warren died in Elmore, Washington, age of 85. The halfway house run by Michael Roody and his family, two years at death, Dick Roody with his mother took charge. Mrs. Roody was left alone with her children to raise. Later, Dick Roody lived in Genesee and had two green tanks in the little house. She owned a delivery stable at that time. A team of horses for hauling would bring a big price in those days. Folks would often stop at night on the halfway house, five miles south of Uniontown. They would often hire a team to take them to where they wanted to go. Now, I've got some more to put on there. You see Todd writing, but I ain't got it on there yet.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: He passed more eventful and varied career than the subject of this sketch, the son of Emerald Isle, and possessing all the fire, energy, skill, I don't know how you pronounce that, and the various channels in his stirring wok, through which, characteristic of his race, which has been manifested in various channels of his stirring wok, through some of the most exciting regions of the world. The western part of the United States, in the last 50 years, our subject came to light one Monday, May 1, 1836, in Condigalaway, Ireland. Being the son of Martin and Mary Faye Tierney, he was educated to 11 years of his age. In need of land and came with an uncle to America, where he attended school for some years, more at that age of 18, started for himself. He commanded operation working in a brickyard, then working in an airy canal during that time, the last enlargement, in that waterway, two years at that time, to find him in the aisleway, St. Louis, then to Mexico, laboring in the various occupations. Then at that time, the gold stampeding Pikes Peak he was with. The first, after excitement, he went to South Lake, driving cattle, then to Kansas, he farmed. That time, then he hired up to ride the Fauna Express, riding from Marysville, Kansas, to Big Sandy, number 3. Six months surfaced him in his dangerous and stirring occupation. Then for one year, he was at less hazardous work of herding horses in that country. Next, he found young attorney freighting from Kansas City to Colorado, and his energy manifested that he gained $25 ,000. In this business, which after the war at Harvard, depreciated nearly 50%, at this time he went in partnership and lost all the major portion of his hard -earned money. It was a great lesson, and he collected his remains together and came west to Nevada, then to California, then on to Oregon, and finally landed loose in 1870. After spending $7 ,000 or more in prospecting, he bought a team and hauled wood, and finally came to the place where he now lives, squatted on a quarter section of land, which he homesteaded, then continued to reside there and improve the area from that time until present, purchasing additional pieces of land for 87 acres, fine fertile land, which is mostly horrendous. Mr. Tierney retired more from the anxious labors. It is of note that Mr. Tierney was one of the first builders, the first telephone line of Fort Scott. In 1866, Mr. Tierney married Miss Mariah Beck in Kansas, and four children were born to them. Thomas Kay married living in Chicago, Jerome married Nora Butler living in Latah County, Anna and Jerome married Nora Butler living in Genesee, Annie M. Tierney, wife of Daniel Haley and living in Genesee, William married Louise Evitz and living in Latah County. Mrs. Tierney died at 74, Mr. Tierney then married again in 1895, and the same year he was called upon again to mourn the death of his second wife, which was a marvelous woman. In 1898 in Howard County, Iowa, for the third time, Mr. Tierney approached the second altar, this time leading to the altars and the drum, and to them had been born one child, Michael J. in early time. Mr. Tierney was active and served as postmaster continuing that capacity by four years. He affiliated the Catholic Church and one of the leading men of his being possessed as there were many qualities to this, with one son, Mike, he died in the March death of a town.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, he was there. They marched 700 of them down there to this camp.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, to Camp O 'Donnell?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, and then Captain O 'Donnell, he wrote to the relatives of what had happened. So they just buried him over there. And then, um...

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Wouldn't he have been a fairly, uh...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: an older man by then? Huh? Wouldn't he have been an older man by then?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, oh yes, he was a young boy, young man. Now, see, that was from Grandpa's third marriage, you know. He stayed single 22 years when his first wife died, and then five or six years after his second wife died. She got out of her trouble. She was a marvelous woman.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That was his son who died?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, Michael. Michael. Yes, mm -hmm.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: He was a sergeant in the office. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: See, what else is there here?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: See, that story I just read, it's coming from a history of North Idaho book. This lady put that in for me. Here's his hero, Admiral Alposition for our energy skill. The war of the Western United States. In the last 50 years, our subject came to life. And his place was, um... He was a great Catholic man, grandfather was, and, uh... He always, his cabin there was a stopping over place for Father Cataldo. And Father Cataldo and the Reverend Henry Spalding were their missionaries in the early days of England, and they converted lots of Indians, and they learned to talk the English language and converted Indians in many ways. And so he made Grandfather's house his stopping place, and he just had services there. And through him, while he helped organize St. Boniface Parish in Uniontown, and my grandfather was one of the organizers there, and he gave the first $75, and there's lots of money for them days. And Father Cataldo used to save services out on the prairies and every place, and then my mother, he suggested that my mother go up here to submit mission. So he took her up there on a little two -wheel cart, and her little belongings, and she stayed there then for four and a half years before she got home again, and they took care of her and learned her a lot of nice things. And then Uncle Will, then he sent Uncle Will, the youngest son, to the Gonzaga College for several years, and that was first organized in AD 6, I think. He was one of the first students there. And that statue of Mr. Monaghan on Riverside Avenue, in Spokane, was one of his schoolmates, classmates. I always heard him tell about that. See, he's passed on too.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, did he know Father Cataldo and...

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, Father Cataldo, he was the first missionary. See, Grandfather, the first one settled over there. Right, right.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did your grandfather know him fairly well?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, yeah, he stayed there all the time when he went through the country, you see. And he would travel through the country and he had a one -horse cart, you know, the little south case, and he'd travel around on that and wind it among the Indians, and him and Reverend Henry Spalding, he was a Presbyterian, and Reverend Henry Spalding was a brother -in -law to Felix Warren, and he settled down here to Spalding. Felix Warren, the stagecoach driver. Yeah, he was related to the stage driver by marriage, and he and Father Cataldo both worked together, Presbyterian and Catholic, and they converted the Indians. They learned to talk Indian. Father Cataldo was an Italian, and it was through Father Cataldo that a lot of this, and Father De Smet, that a lot of this community was built up, you know, through those two priests, you see, and they helped build the churches around this community, and so many of the old -timers here had built the churches, old dad and a whole lot of them, and when they go under, they want a party for the news about the church and everything, and I gave them a lot about the churches here, too, you see, and this church up here was, of course, burned in 1950, I think it was, the old building, the first building, and then my dad helped build that, and they hauled rock clear from Polk and Uniontown to build a church and all things like that, and so they...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did your grandfather ever tell you how Father Cataldo looked or acted or something like that?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I see him. He used to be here, and he's a little old, stooped -over man, and late years, when I was in school here in Genesee, well, they had a...

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: what do they call that now?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: They call it a retreat or something, and he and a whole lot of other priests were in, so curiosity, I got to go up there, and so he was stooped -over little old man, and he spoke Latin and everything, well -spoken, but he could hardly talk, and I remember him, and he helped build that, he built a church in Lewiston, Idaho, St. Sandiswap Church, he built that there, too, and he worked hard among the Indians, and he slept out on the quilts and out on the prairie and everything, and the Indians, he was a great friend of the Indians. He just traveled around by himself, with no escort or anything. Oh yes, with this little cart, and he'd stay with the people, what few people there were, you know, and them days, while people had cabins and homemade beds and everything, he'd be glad enough to sleep on the floor just so they had a bed to sleep on, you know, and the Indian Cataldo were ferocious, you know, off a bed, and grandfather, you know, his place was always, he'd always welcome to everybody, and any time of the night or any time, they always kept their door locked, you know, and they'd have to give full identification before they could enter the house.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Is that because of the Indians, or what?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, the Indians were ferocious, you know, the Indians might break in on you and just take everything you got, and I know my mother told me one time, she used to tell me so much history about the country, you know, and during the Indian War, the Indians killed all one family down in this part someplace there, and when they were in the war, and they killed all the family, and one girl, she was left tied to a post, and there was a dagger driven through her tongue, and they took and they got, saved her, the people did, and they trimmed her tongue off around, and for a long time she used to be down there at the fairs, and I knew her name, but I wish I wrote it down now, and they'd have her at the fair there, and she had long brown hair, and she was nice looking, she could talk after her tongue was trimmed, and the Indians used to fish along the Snake River there, Lewiston, you know, and Mr. Damus used to have a store there, and Amy Damus was his daughter, and my mother used to stay with Mrs. Carney, and they used to fish along the river there, and all they'd wear was just a piece of canvas, or a piece of buckskin around their body, you know, so Amy Davis used to tease the old little squawings, you know, and they'd just take Amy by the neck and throw her out and scare my mother nearly to death, and they used to have ketchup stand in there and everything like that, and my mother used to enjoy that, and then when she stayed with Mrs. Carney for a while, I feel that she was about four years old, she went up to, grandfather took her up to Moscow, Idaho, and you see a street up the name Deacon, well that's at her culture site, Jim's Deacon and his wife homesteaded that, and grandfather left mother there, and while she was staying there, she fell back in a pot of boiling brine, and she cried for six weeks, it was terrible, and the neighbors, the Snow brothers up there, the Snow's, it was their grandmother, or great grandmother, and all the kind neighbors tried to help poor Mrs. Deacon all they could, and for six weeks my mother suffered, and so she wasn't making progress in healing the wounds, so Grandma Snow had the boys kill a coyote, and she rendered that tallow out, and she put that over, instead of grafting skin like I do nowadays, she put that there, foreign tallow, all over her little body, and she healed up, but her body was marred, I could always see where she was burned, you know, and so then they didn't take care of her anymore, but it was about that time, and then Mr. McConnell had a McConnell store, you know, and Manchin's named after, he had a store there in Moscow, and he was a good looking man, and my mother used to go, and I have a brooch that I have stored away, that Mr.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: McConnell gave my mother when she was a young woman, and Grandfather used to take her up there, and bought her a wedding outfit and everything up there, and he was awful proud of her, you know, and when Mother come home from school, my Grandfather had the man picked up for her, because he had this acreage, you know, of course he was 24 years old, and my mother, that don't go good, you know, and he said he didn't want any young bucks hanging around there, so anyway, my mother and Dad married, and Grandfather had a wedding that, your grandfather had kind of picked out your dad?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, and so he told her to take her home and raise her the way you want her, she learned to milk eight cows.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Your mother was 16 then?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Wasn't 16 yet, oh, it's a good old, and so then, anyhow, after they were married, well just before I was born, my Dad fell off my haystack and broke his leg, and they couldn't save, the bone was so shattered, and they were milking eight cows, and he was hauling milk to Genesee, first milkman, you know, 1887 to 1890, and that kind of broken, had a lot of chickens, and they had 30 head of cattle, and had a hard man there and everything, and the snow was real deep that wonder, so they took Dad to the Sacred Heart Hospital, one of the first patients there, and they amputated his leg, and he came back, and he had a peg leg for 27 years, and then finally got an artificial leg, and I used to help him and everything, and he had nothing new for him to come over the hills, a big band of hogs, and he made all the ranks of hog ties, and they built a new hog house to save himself, to get out of his ditch, and he succeeded, and hogs went down to one and a half cents a pound on the foot.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When was that?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, that was in 1896 and 1997, and Dad got, he didn't get discouraged, he got on the buckskin cayuse and he drove those hogs over, and he turned them loose, and he built a nice new hog house, and put a vat in there, and a fire and everything, and one wonder, he put you in a whole carload, and he cured those hogs dry, cured them, you know, and he made four and a half cents on them, by doing so, and I used to have to help him round those hogs up, we had two good dogs, and I was just a little kid, seven years old, and my goodness, that was awful on me, you know, and so later years, why then, when we moved to town, we moved in the house up there, Dad built, and so when the school age come, while we moved to town to go to school, when I was about 14 years old, my mother and I went out to the ranch, you know, Dad wasn't home that day, and so there was an old hog and a bunch of little pigs, and the little pigs, all run down, crawled in a hole in the middle, you know, and the biggest one was out, and I grabbed him by the foot, and we put him in a gun sack, and carried him two and three fourth miles to town, we raised him, and that's what we did, you know, and then another time, I was about 16, we walked out the ranch, and we got in the smokehouse, and she carried a ham, and I carried a shoulder, and carried him two and three fourth miles to Genesee, tell you, I felt sore for several days, it was awful, and then here in Genesee, in earlier days, why, they used to have meat markets and everything like that, and when we was on the ranch, Nagel and Baumberg had the butcher shop, and they made the best bologna in the country, all the young people used to think they were great, so, old Charlie Baumberg, he was an old, kind of a fresh old guy, and always liked to be nice to ladies, so I come out to the woman, we were working for Mother, and we went to sell some meat, he had a little wagon, you know, two red ponies hooked up, and they delivered meat to the farmers in harvest days, and he went to put his arm around her, and she hit him on the head with a milk pan, she doesn't stand for it, so Nagel and Baumberg made the finest bologna as we thought, and he had a butcher shop here, and behind that butcher shop, over high lowered fence, he had a treadmill, and he had two horses, and they would work that with their feet, and then they had a rod going from that going into the butcher shop, and it was cocked wheels, and they grinded their sausage that way, oh, that was a grinder, yes, that was a grinder, like they used to try sweet here in the early days, and see in the early days, why they didn't have the traction machines, you know, they had to frele their wheel out to take it to the secretary of the union, to take it down, you know, and the cradle that daddy used was a little side, you know, and you know what they are, and then they had these slats on, and that's what dad could, obviously, first crop with.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, the way he worked that is, is you'd side it down and fall in the cradle?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, I just fall on the slats, and then he'd gather bundles and tie it together with a natural straw. Oh, when he got a full cradle's worth. Yes, and that's the way he did it, of course he had two legs, and he was a thrifty strong man, he worked awfully hard, and then they had that, and that's the way they harvested, when first harvest, they had to frele their grinder out, well the next first machine that came out was a horse power, and there'd be twelve horses, and there'd be two of them, and a bunch, and they'd travel over this rod, and this rod would have clogged wheels around the traction machine, and we used to call them coffee mills, and that's the way the thrashing grandmother would have ten or twenty men to cook for, and how she done it, I don't know, that little, and other women the same way.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And, uh, Was it when you were still in the smaller house, or in the bigger house?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, that one was out on the ranch then, where my mother lived then, they built a big house out there, and after my mother and my dad, he built a big house. I was born in a rusty cabin, that's where I was born, and they just burned that down lately, that was built with iron nails, and then, uh, they thrashed that, and they'd come next to the big steam engine, and they was pulled by six horses, and they burned straw in that, or a straw burner they called it. And then the next thing come the wood burner, and they kept the fireman busy poking wood in, and the header used to come, they cut for the binder first, and then the next one was a header. And, uh, so then mother had seven men to cook for, then them days, and I tell you the girls knew how to cook, and how to get in, and cook, and milk cows and everything, you know, we had to. And just the children, them days were too busy, and they had plenty to do, and I'll tell you, we just didn't have no time to fool around.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Didn't cause much trouble then, huh?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, and we had to cause, um, we had to grind all our coffee by hand, a little coffee mill, you know, and everything, and mother put me to grinding coffee, that one I was pretty small, it was pretty hard going, you know.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And did you ever read that story about Captain Brennan, 31 years on the plains? Oh, Wild Davey, you heard about him?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes. Well, he worked for us.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Really?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, I've got his book here. Huh. And so, dad couldn't get nobody to help him out there, my youngest sister was just a little tiny baby about three months old.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And when we heard Wild Davey was coming.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: He was a man, he knew Wild Bill Hickoff, didn't he?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, when we heard Wild Davey was coming, we was scared to death. Wild Davey, so we watched, and he come in the gate, and here he was cleaned up. He had blue jeans on, blue overhauls, and a splat shirt, and he had two little braids on his back, and bangs. And he was cleaned up, and he come in and he worked there about a week. And he used to tell us stories about his days, how he run away from home, and all this and that. And he was writing this book. And Mrs. Clyde borrowed the book to read, and mother told me, she saw him in Spokane on the street corner selling his little books. So I went to John W. Gray in Spokane, I bought the book. And I paid only a dollar a car for another seven dollars, eight dollars in the bookstore, you see. And that was Wild Davey for you. So after I come up here with the pioneers, why the women up there were telling me about him, how dirty he was, and he had three dogs, and they never washed his plates, the dogs lick the plates off. And all said, well he lived on a well, he lived in a well, you know, and he carries a little neck, a knapsack on his back. So he used to tell us about, when he was traveling around, why he was up in Sandpoint, Idaho, in his younger days. And he come in contact with a banker up there, and his wife and him got real nice to him. So he and the banker said, oh, invite him up to his house. And he said he liked, he says, told Bill to try and get some bar meat for him, some bear, you know, that bar meat he called. And he said, oh, he says, I can get you some. He says, I can't take it home and cook it. Well, he says, my wife won't have it in the house. Well, he says, I'll tell you what I'll do, you now tell her, I'll get you a steak and bar meat, and we'll take it up to the house and she can cook it. So he says, I got a nice big steak and bar meat, and we took it up to the house and she cooked that and it might smell good. And he says, my, she says, have a taste of dog. That was nice meat, and banker, oh, he enjoyed it too. So after all got up soon, everything is ready to be, he says, well, I'll miss this. He says, don't ever tell me you never eat bar meat. He says, that was bar meat you eat, and she run him out of the house. She was so mad. The other day, we never saw him after that, but he always had dogs, but he didn't have his dogs out to the ranch. But so many of the ladies up in Moscow told me about him, you know, how he used to go around and leave the dogs, and he'd come and ask for something neat and everything, but he never did that with us, you know. But anyway, why, he was quite a guy, I tell you.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where did he live around, where did he live? Huh? Where did he live?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Out in the wild or stay with people, or ask him to stay, you see, he had about two or three dogs.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, was he helping with harvest or what?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, he come live around, I'll say, sorry, Terry Waters, Terry Waters, he had feet from the house, and his mother had a little baby, she didn't need no lady to help her, so there was four of his children, you know, and it was pretty hard, but she's afraid of the woman. She had a harvester to cook for and they kept her there for a week. Yeah, that was it. But I learned to cook, I used to like to cook for harvesters, and when I first moved in here, for two years, it took me a long time to get used to, when I see the green tanks come in, you know, I'd get homesick for the harvesters to cook for them. And I lived in Spokane twelve years, you know, after we left the ranch, after I was seventeen, I left for Spokane, I lived up there, we had our home up there, and my husband was a construction worker for the Spokane Traction Company one time, so my father gave us off and we come back down on the ranch, and I stayed there fifty years, and work, oh my, I raised eight hundred and five geese all the years I lived there, and I've got pictures of them, they're just beautiful.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Eight hundred and five altogether?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, the whole eight years, all the years I was there. And I had down pillows for all my girls to repair, and feather mattresses, and I had orders for eleven orders before I quit, and my hands just got so I couldn't work anymore, I just got tired out.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Too much for me.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kind of geese did you raise?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I had some big white geese, the big wild geese, the big gray geese, and then I had some big white ones too. And I've had, carrying those feathers and everything, and I dressed every one, one year I had sixty -nine, and I killed them and dressed them all myself. And I used to do, I just loved poultry, you know, and animals and everything, and there was never a year hardly ever that I would have had a pet pig. And I used to take the pet pigs, you know, the old hog, you know, sometimes they'd be crippled or something, and I'd pick them up and I'd start them out, you know, nearly dead, and I'd make a, I'd have a whole land of a box, and I'd have them put the net on through there, so they, they looked for their feed, you know, and I was all about overfeeding them and everything. So the girls from the university called me up one time and asked me if they could have a pig up there for a mascot. I said, well that thing would raise the whole campus, I said, we need to get out of here, I wouldn't do that. Oh, they were interesting, you can teach a hog there, and she got so, we had twenty -eight head hogs one time, and she used to walk up to the garden with me, there was a little white hog, you know, she weighed about a pretty near three hundred, and so she'd follow me all over, and so one time I was going to the garden, the hogs were out there eating grass, you know, and they all tried to look at her and she stuck her nose up in the air and wouldn't even recognize them, putting in mind to people, you know, how they'll do with a stick of a nose over nature, like just the way she acted. Oh, they can be a nuisance too, and the girls come to see us, you know, they'd have white shoes on her nose, they'd root around the white shoes. Yeah, we had quite a time. Yes, that was a way of it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was the area like when you were young? Was it still pretty unsettled?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, there was more population and fully settled, and there was more people living in these small farms, forty acre tracts, and now they all, big farmer bought the little fella out, just like the place of the business, and forty acre tracts, and I can name it out there, now there's here four farms, I've lent big farmers, I've kept buying these parcels of land, you know, and that's big farmers owning it, and in the early days it was a walking plow and a riding plow, then the gang plow come in, and people were, Mike Ewits and my grandfather and all of them could have had all kinds of land, but they were afraid it was too much for them because they only had the horses, you see, to farm it, and nowadays they'd grab it up by land, my dad had a place bought here for a dollar and a quarter of an acre at one time, and then he bought another, then he let it go because he thought he had enough, he had two hundred and forty acres, and then there was different places, you know, that could have been bought, now it's four and five hundred dollars an acre, and they had to use fertilizer, and my uncle, he died, and he used to prophesize all things that had happened, and I think he was talking through his hat, so he said, now you watch now, I may not be here to see it, but he says this land here is going to be sold, it won't produce, naturally, it'll have to be fertilizers and stuff, well first he talked about the planes, there'll be chariots floating through the air without horses, and I thought he was bugs, and first he said, you know, the planes come, and he talked about the automobiles, and then he talked about the land, and my goodness, it's coming to us, all this fertilizer and stuff has to be fertilized, and they're trying to find something new, my grandson, he's a graduate of the State College of Washington, and he's in charge of all these little plots, experimental plots, and they're trying to find out new things, new grains, his professor invented this game, wheat, that's good wheat, and they're trying to find out new things to do, and then my daughter, my granddaughter, his sister, is on the staff in the research department, the horticultural, and they go on up and find out all the diseases, the potatoes and all things like that, and she's working in the office there, and so this research, and then her folks have this beautiful dairy down here, outside of Colton, you know, well that's my daughter that lives there, they have 30 Guernsey cows, and they were awarded prizes and first rates, top rating, first plaque, perfect plaque for having perfect cleanliness and everything, for Guernsey cows, you can walk in that barn, and you can see a bit of dirt and nothing, it's just perfectly clean, and so quite clean inside there, and then they had all these cows there and everything, and you go inside there, and you never know there was cows in there, it's just lovely in there, and they go, Emonia's family had a nice family, and the daughter lives in Moscow, and then the other one, her husband was in the service, and they just got back from Germany, now they're living in the Clarkson Heights, and my, the interesting places they were over in there, Germany, there was, her daughter was born in Heidelberg, Germany, and they're Italy, and they were in Italy and France and Switzerland, and they wrote all kinds of stories about that, and really interesting, and she can tell you all kinds, she sent me things from Germany there, well then, my youngest daughter, that's my second most daughter, my youngest daughter, my oldest daughter lives in Seattle, and her husband was a high grade people, and she's from, she worked at the Grange as a secretary about six years and a half, and then she went over to Seattle, she was a timekeeper at the port of embarkation, then auditing, and then the other one, she was teaching for 13 years now, she went down to San Francisco and taught down at Morial School, Morial School, and then her husband, her teaches in Seattle now, and then my grandson, he's 19 years old, and the Washington State Band was chosen to play inaugural parade, and he's in that band, 102 pieces, he got to march in that band, and he's quite a genius, she's doing lots of things, and the little girl too, she got five awards for her schooling in Seattle, and she, she's quite a vocalist and a piano player, she's taken piano now about 10 years, she's really good, and they come up here and they work around here and they do wonderful things for me, they're all so busy and so active, well then when they celebrate the heritage of the first pioneers here, they all surprised me, they were here, they took all kinds of pictures of us and everything, and I read a story that a copy from, my oldest daughter fixed it, and modeled it for me, I think I can, I got it in that book there, some people got it, I'll take it off of this, I took it from a story written by John Naylor, Tells of Early Day Settlers, contributed by Mrs. H .B. Jones, and here I'll read this off, what Mr. Naylor wrote, that was Mrs., I guess you know who that was, Roy Naylor's father, the paper prepared by John L. Naylor, President of the Lito County Pioneers in 1920, throws much light on early settlers, the county, its formation, its first officer's history of Moscow, the county seat, Moscow at this time has a population of 4200, and the seat of the University of Idaho, the state's largest educational institution, Mr. Naylor was an early day settler in Lito County, and chairman of the first board of county commissioners, he has kept a careful record of chronological events in this county, this paper follows, as to who was the first settlers in Lito County, it would be a matter of congestion from miners, and trappers were here for different periods before any attempt to build settlement made, John B. Cannon settled in Moscow in August 1865, and my grandfather, he was my grandfather's great friend, Mr. Buchanan, he raised vegetables, hogs, and chickens, and marketed them at Lucen, and supplied miners who were prospecting or washing the Houdoun Upper Blue Puce River, in 1865 Frank Poynes, Ladd Miller, Arthur Green, Charlie Hunter, William King, and Longline Lockridge were the northern part of Lito County, Thomas, Tierney, and Family settled in Thorne Creek in September 1870, at that time there were no white settlers near him, the innards were ferocious, and friendly, and used to dig their winter supply of canvases on the flat lands where the wonder grew through the county, early in 1871 -72 the actual settlers who had located around Moscow began to come in, the sod was easily broken, and produce a good crop, and little, can't see what that is, a little breaking before that time, and wheat became the modern medium of exchange, after wheat growing became an industry, three bushes of wheat were exchanged for a day's work in Harvest Field, the medium of exchange was trade, store and jawbone, my old friend Thomas Tierney always tells the way, he made his money by buying on jawbone and selling for cash, provisions were not plentiful, at Lewiston in Moscow, Mr. Tierney would drive a team and wagon over the Puce, Washington for flour, coffee, sugar, and brown, it was hard to get dried apples by the barrel, so was sunshine syrup, two and three gallon jackets, often times he was caught in a snowstorm when he slept under his wagon at night, he never seemed to have trouble with the innards, and Mrs. Tierney used to say, give them food and we will have no trouble, the prairie chickens were very plentiful, Mrs. Tierney built the first cabin, and the present buildings are part of the estate remains in the family today, Tom Tierney welcomed all travelers in his home for shelter, on their way from Puce to Lewiston for food, the first school built of logs in 1875 set on the place, formed by Lewis Herman, they moved within a quarter of a mile from Tierney's, Mr. Tierney served on the school board many years before moving to Moscow in 1901, he lived until August 24, he died at 84 years old, he was active in the last take care of his garden, going to town, my daughter fixed this for me,

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well your grandfather and grandmother then welcomed the Indians so they would stay friends with them? Oh yes, always did, grandfather had no trouble with them, and grandmother was a congenial person, she always got along with everybody that way, that's what I was told, Well why was it then that they wanted your mother to go away, why wasn't they afraid for her safety? No, after grandmother died you see, well they had to be somebody to raise my mother you know, see grandmother died when my mother was two years old, That was the people in Lewiston who wanted her to go away, or they wanted to take her someplace else?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, Father Cataldo thought it was the best place for her to go to a girls school, and then he suggested for her to go to Smith Mission up here, and then he took her up on a little two wheeled cart, It took him three days to go up there, and it rained all three days, and the people were very accommodating on the way up, they'd let them stay overnight and things like that,

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: My husband was probably helping a neighbor to find some grain, and he was going to come home in the evening, and I expected the crew the next day to have a day's work, And a lot of days work to finish up, oats and things, and I got all ready, and here he come, the man himself come there about three o 'clock, and he says, Say he says I can come in here now if I can come in and trash your oats, and I said well, I said I'm not ready until tomorrow but I have to go to town and get some things for me, And he said well I can come in, he isn't here just now, but I says well do you know where the sacks are, I can get them, I'll go over in the field and we'll have supper here, Oh I says I'm not ready, so I thought the next morning I'd go and get my stuff, and my goodness I had supper to get, and he says well you'll have to wait a week longer, well they didn't look too good, so I thought well I can get supper, Alright I'll just let them come in, so they got the sacks, and the two girls were home, they were kind of small yet, and I sent one up in the orchard to get apples, and I made applesauce first thing, I run back and got potatoes, and I made hot biscuits, And I had a lot of canned sausage and stuff in the cellar, in the basement, corn and everything, and by 7 .30, I sent the other girls a neighbor to get a pound of coffee, and by that 7 .30 I had dinner ready for 11 men, didn't take me long, What did you give them, the applesauce? Oh I had potatoes and vegetables and salads and everything, I used to give them plenty to eat, always, I would say we had our own cows and butter and cottage cheese and everything, and chickens you know, and then I'd make this sausage in the wintertime, We'd butcher you know, and I'd make a sling sausage and smoke it, and then I'd pack it in jars, and I always had something there, I could fix it in a hurry, it never bothered me to cook for 10 or 14 at one time, but I can't do it now anymore, I'm past that, Was it a pretty rough bunch of men that worked on thrashing crews, or was it?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, it depended who they were, some of them were very nice, now one time my dad, I had some there, and I guess he got away with the leader lines, and stole his double barrel shotgun self loader, and some things like that, but they're all pretty respectable, mostly local people you know, But the strangers of course they slept outside in the barns and things like that, but they'd come in and eat and they're all actually decent, I used to like to cook for them though, they never minded a bit at all, when they were hardy eaters you know, but if they'd be finicky then I didn't like that.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Really?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I wouldn't think after working that hard all day they'd be anybody finicky. Well they really liked their food, they really did, they used to have to take hot lunches out too, men you know, and quarter time, but they don't do that anymore, and they had cookshacks you know, and after they'd done all the cookshacks, the farmers' wives had to cook for the men you know, and a lot of women didn't like it, but now women up to date, they've got to take out these two potluckies, and they're nearly killed. So they don't have those nice gatherings anymore, and that's just too bad. Genesee's had a lot, it was a population, it was a busy place, and I remember we had Chinese laundries here, I had two of them, and they had saloons, one saloon, let's see two saloons and one brewery, and Matt Cambridge used to make a beer.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh and one brewery?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, and he had the brewery, and he used to make it out of the malt barley.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You said there was Matt Cambridge?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, Matt Cambridge, K -M -B -I -T -S -C -S -H, and he had the best beer in town, and people used to take a little five pound pail and go down and get a bucket of beer for fifteen cents, and they used to do all kinds of things, like we had restaurants here, and I remember my mother lived here, I used to take butter downtown, sell it for twelve and a half cents a pound, two or twenty -five cents a roll, and then I'd haul chickens downtown, nice big hens, twenty -five cents a piece, and a little wagon, and I was only twelve, thirteen years old, I went out and made the sales, I got along alright, and when we lived on the ranch, I'd walk in there twenty -four miles and I'd carry a couple dozen eggs, and you could buy calico for five cents a yard, enough to make a dress, and everything was cheap then, you know, and people would go in and decide the grocery store, you'd smell everything, it smelled so good, you know, and they'd have these little loon fish there on the bales, and they were very particular, but I think old Jay Kloseenstein had his store, and he had a cave on the wall, and he used to hang his, what they call sal bale up on the ceiling, and only it has the butter in it, and it had a cupboard with a screen on it, and of course the butter had all the taste of the meats and things like that, but people bought it, and they never hollered or anything.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And there was lots of bachelors around the country, too, and the Indians, they used to call it the Indian Reservation, when I first came back here, I talked about the Indian Reservation, well, people didn't know what I was talking about, well there was always out there a lot of squawmen, and a lot of other good families living out there, you know, and Up towards Fixerage or what?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Noralisa Genesee, several miles up from, now it's mostly all people that have, that's the Indian line that have been bought up from the government relief and things like that, but I went to school with some of those girls, and the Indians are good people, and this way, if they think you're their friend, like Mother always used to say, if they think, that was her experience, that was at the mission, it was half Indian and half white girls, and they had them in the separate building, and Mother said if they think you're their friend, they're good, but she said, let them find that you're not their friend, you're the little couches, they don't like you. No, it's awful. And when the Indians, during the Indian War, would scowl the people, they'd just take off, just the hair off of here, you see, and Mother said they had to take that to the Chief to prove that they got a white man. Oh yeah, they were kind of mean, and they were scared to death of the Indians, but she used to always tell them not to be afraid of them, and so many of these new people come here, why, from the eastern states, why they say the Indians, they'd get so scared and so frightened, they'd make fun of them and everything, why it was awful the way they used to talk about them, and rows of Indians used to go through here, and they'd carry their parthenails, and go through and go up huckleberry picking, and gather up stuff for the winter, you know, and it was nothing new to see Indians on the canyons getting up all the wild roots for, feed for the winter, and then the Indians used to get their deer hide, and oh, any hide, they'd get a hold of them, they'd leave a decay in the air so long, and then they had a kind of gadget, bone -like, and they'd take all the hair off of that, and then they would let that hide dry, and they'd shoot over their teeth, and it would make it soft to make mucks and things out of, that's the way they did. But you don't ever see anything like that anymore now, there's going to be so few of them, you know. I used to like to watch the Indians, they'd just go and deal with them, they'd have no trouble with them, they'd get your pigs cheep from them, you see.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kind of town was Genesee?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Was it a pretty quiet town, or was it a pretty... Oh, they had every rules they had to live up to.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: They had the Methodist church, the Congregational church, and the Christian church, the Lutheran church, and the Catholic church, and the Danish church, and that church up in the valley there is the oldest church, the oldest building standing now. It's in Lutheran. Yes, that's Lutheran, and that's an awful nice little church inside, and then they had, this town was quiet, they had doctors here, two doctors, and they had a dentist, and a lawyer, and a real estate man, a millinery store, and two nice merchandise stores that I'm a member of, and it was a real nice, awfully nice, restaurants. Now, I guess I don't think there's even a restaurant here, now I think that closed up. We don't even have a drug store, and we had a drug store, and Dr. Cornyn and his son, they built a nice building, and the fraternal orders used to meet upstairs, you know, and old Dr. Cornyn had beautiful white hair, and white beard, and he always kept his floor oiled. So one certain man went in one time and spit on the floor in front of the counter, and he just walked right in front of the counter to piss chocolate and mark a circle right around, he was so mad. He didn't like it at all. You know, they were good, we had pretty good doctors at that time, but now, I don't know, I was about 12 years old, that one doctor, he operated on my one eye, and he cut ulcers out, and I said, take it, you only use cocaine, I couldn't stand that now. No, I had no full time. Everything was so free and so open, and such clean town, the air, the atmosphere here was so clean and pure, you know, and not like it is now, and we used to have such snow storms here. Oh, it used to be so, they could drive over the hills and over the fences and everything. Now, I don't know where the snow went. One year we did have a lot of snow, I've taken pictures of the ranch out there, we had a lot of snow. And there's awful lot of snow, and I was a little girl, we used to go out coasting all the time, we used to have lots of fun. I'd invite the school kids out there, you know, and they'd have toboggan parties and everything, but there's no snow now, much. They don't know, kids don't know what a good time is.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What did you do for a good time when you were a kid?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, we rode horses and we went out sleigh riding and we cut pictures out of books, and mother used to, we were satisfied, and we'd go out and play, you know, and things like that, and we had pets, and then of course school, and had activities then, but I don't know, the kids nowadays have to get in the car and have to race up and down, and I don't know what they're doing.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: We had a ... and everybody had to be in by 8 o 'clock, and they were strict, and I remember the old lamp lighter that used to be here, we had those posts on the corners, you know, and they had the carbon lights, you know, and used to light, and he used to get up on little step lighter and light those lights, and then there was a pound here, a pound here, they had back a delivery barn, and anybody's cow would get out, why?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: He'd put the cow in, cost a dollar to get her out, and that's the way they used to do. Oh yeah, they did everything.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did your grandfather ever tell you why he decided to settle out here, and why he would go so far away from everybody else to settle into a place?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, I didn't come out to settle out, I didn't homestead or anything. No, your grandfather. Yeah, well, grandfather came out here because the country was so open, and he wasn't the only one.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: There's the Butchers, and the Scharnhaus, and the, and Mike Evitts was next, he was in 1870, Stravitz, and Your grandfather was the first one to settle in a thorn creek in 1870, he'd come down from Moscow, and he started in here, and there was a deep creek, and it was nice clean water, and there used to be crawfish and everything, I remember all the thorn bushes was there, and then Butchers right above him, you know, and they all worked together, the pioneers, and they were so accommodating, the pioneers were kind, you know, to one another, and always helped one another, and my grandmother's sister and her husband, Mr. and Mrs. Jim Hansen, come out from back east, from back in Kansas, where they settled first when they come out from Denmark, and they walked up the Lewiston hill, and they carried their oldest baby, and carried their lunch, and they come up the top of the hill and hired a team of high prize, and come over to her sister's place in Thorn Creek, stay there, Mrs. Thomas Tierney, and they settled there for a whole few months, and they thought they liked Genesee country better, so they came here and settled over here in Genesee, and right on top of the hill, you can see them trees, and they settled here, and they lived here, and they had three sons and a daughter, and the daughter died in childhood days, and my grandmother and she were sisters, and she was always a great lady, like my grandmother, always willing to help others, so grandma Hansen always went out, any time of the night, or any time when she was called, and ride bareback horse rain or sunshine, and help people in maternity cases, and the sickness, and she was awful kind to everyone, she was a wonderful woman.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: She was kind of the midwife around here?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, she helped a lot, and so, she, and they lost their daughter then, and they settled over here, and then their house burned, they had misfortune, but that didn't discourage them any, and he homesteaded the land here, and they belonged to fraternal organizations, and they used to go drive down Lewiston, stay overnight, and attend all the parties, and have a good time, great for Rebecca, as you know, and odd fellows, and they used to entertain, and grandpa Hansen told me that they used to have the best times when they were young, they believed in having a good time, not accumulating a lot, you know, well that was a good way to be, and lots of people, and used to have these country dances, you know, you heard of them, you know, where groups would gather, you know, and they'd dance up two o 'clock in the morning, and everybody would carry a grub alone, and we'd have a good time, play harmonica, and violin, you know, people had good times, and then they'd give dances here in Genesee, certain parties would give dances in Genesee, and any girl going out of the hall, during a dance hall with a fellow, they were talked about, they had to stay inside, wasn't decent, you know, and the guys would chew clothes to kill the whiskey, smell all their breath, oh, and then sometimes they'd have supper, some organization would give supper, no two bits of meal, and our boyfriend would take us to the dinner, you know, we used to enjoy it very much, we had good times, and then of course I left here when I was about 17, and I went to work in Spokane, and I worked up there until I was married, and come back down here, I seemed over good to get down here on this black soil, or to sit, dry, clean and wholesome, you know, because of city life, I wasn't for that, I liked animals, and I had success with that, milking cows, and raising calves, and pigs, and chickens, and poultry, and I used ducks and chickens, and rabbits and everything, you know, I did an awful lot of that, and then I had butter, I used to make butter, I had customers here in town for years, I always had something to sell off of the ranch, and then I used to ship quite a bit to Spokane, too, because I lived up there, and I knew who to ship to, I'd write and ask, you know, so it changed quite a bit then, Now I was always wanting to be up in front and take the lead in lots of things, and I got along alright, I liked it, we had good schools here, and I praised the University of Idaho, they sure were nice to me, my daughter had the large agent on, Miss Richie and Miss Nelson, they helped me, and she belonged to 7 years of 4 -H, and she wanted a trip to Chicago, and so did that woman in Colton, too, they both got trips to the 4 -H club, and so Miss Richie says it's a shame to let the town go to waste, and so I just worked out a plan for her with a diet, and she's glad enough to eat enough, and she's in the kitchen no matter what she had, so she got her diet, and she helped work her way through school now, she's one of the best teachers over in New Orleans, she's part time teacher at the community college, and she has a little business of her own, her and another lady, and they make
