these ski suits, you know, and so they get together on that for a sideline, and she goes down to Portland and gets her materials, pre -ordered from the people, and she gets $10 for these, remember she had them at 10 or 20, and she made a lot of money last year, and then they were bringing the sewing machine company over for a demonstration, she gets $10 in order for that, you know, she's busy, too, awful busy, so that's the way young people do, they want to get out and do it, you know, and 4 -H club, I tell you that 4 -H club is a fine foundation for young people, I think it's wonderful that they stay with it, never drop out, and of course you got to take the better of the sweet, but stay with it, that's what I say, we have good leaders here, too, and the girls did the work on the ranch, helped me on the ranch and everything, it took time for 4 -H, just always keeping busy, that's what my daughter always said, keep them busy, and they never give them mischief, you know, so she keeps her children busy, too, so I feel happy about my children, grandchildren, they've all done alright.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Um, one question I had here, I was wondering, when you were talking about when Tom Tierney built his first cabin, the hideout, did he have to haul the logs for that, too?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, they had to go up in the mountains, but there was timber clearing to Troy, you know, and Moscow, lots of timber around here them days.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: About how far, how long a haul was it?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, they'd take care of their, what they call the grub wagon, they'd take their wagon and they'd go up there, and the team was running gears of the wagon and they'd go up there and hew the logs out and bring them down here, and Palouse, Washington was the nearest place to get provisions, but they couldn't get in Lewiston, Lewiston had nothing there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was it just a couple miles, or 5 miles, or 10 miles?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, 20 miles, 21 miles, all of that, oh yes, people them days lived out in the open, that's what mediums are tough, I think. Yes, that was it. So, anyway, why, then we had the churches here, you know, on Sunday evening at 7 .30, there'd always be churches, you know, and so we used to go to, Mother and us girls used to go to all the churches and hear the sermons, you know, whatever, Sunday evening, and I remember one minister, he was an evangelist traveling around, and he was telling about, you know, he used to roll cigarettes them days, and he was telling about a funny story, he says, now I want to tell you folks something for your own good health, he said, don't chew tobacco, and he says, don't smoke, that's for injuries to health, and cigarettes are cask and nails. That was a good answer. And then all we told about different things, I don't know, these, and there's so many of these people, I can count them down to five generations before, and then we go and get a marriage, I got to stop and think of their relation. I knew, there were such big families them days, but nowadays there's only two and three, maybe none at all now, you know, it's just different. I seen all these houses go up around here, I remember all of them, and so many of the older people in that generation are gone. My schoolmates, I was 17 in the class, and I can count three here, that's all, living.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Who were those three?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Kate Baumgartner and Christina Jacobs, and Mrs. John Cluss.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Christina Jacobs?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Mrs. John Cluss.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: K -L -U -S?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, S -S.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did, how was your family affected by the First Depression, the 1893 Depression, was that hard on you out here?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, my father and mother, see, Dad rented the ranch out, and that's how, his renter couldn't pay the rent, that's why he had to move back, you see, they run these hogs up and make good, you see, the renter couldn't pay rent, it just rained so much that fall, that was an unusual year, it rained so much that the stacks turned green, you couldn't get them thrashed. And them days they put it in stacks when they headed it, and they had a derrick table that had four things in it, and that was a derrick, that was a derrick top for these. Them days the thrashing machines would crawl between the stacks, and then they'd have to have this derrick table, no, the derrick table would crawl between the stack and the thrashing machine beside that, and the derrick table would have these derricks, you know, the forks, and it had to be covered, and load them up and haul up the derrick table, and we'd move them into cylinders and the thrashing machine, that's where the thrash are green. And that's what they had those days, and of course, a few people had to hit her then, it was actually them days, and they had to wait for the thrashing, the thrashing didn't commence, so they lost the grain. So Dad took the ranch back again, and so we moved back there, and we stayed there for, oh, I don't know how many years. That's when they turned the hogs loose into the... And that's when they turned the hogs into green stacks, that's when they turned, that's all that saved him that time. He went in debt too, he had to go in debt, you know, to get started over again, but he made it though. Dad was sharp, and he was interesting to talk to, and when he was living here in town, oh, I used to go up and keep a house for him for four winters, and I'd send the girls to school. And I used to hear people come up at night, and I'd hear them talking about these pioneer stories, and I could jog down a lot of them. And then they used to get in fights in earlier days, you know, and some of the old fellas, you know, used to carry blackjack, you know what that is. That's a buckskin sack with buckshot in it. And then when they get mad and get drunk and have a fight, they'd hit them in the face with it. That's what they had. And I knew, by names, knew the people personally that had those old fellas, you know, they're all gone now, of course. On Sunday in harvest time, they used to have so many, as I say, they used to come and do their shopping on Sunday, that's the only time. And the stores would all stay open, and you'd go past the brewery and, oh, we'd go with our heads straight, and you'd hear all the thrashes in there having a good time singing, drinking, and fooling around, you know. And then they'd all come home with a header box full of watermelons and have a good time. Then the next day, why, they'd all be to work again. That's the way they would do. You know, they, uh, the town was awful lively then. And the merchants did the land office business, and they always looked forward to the people coming in.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think thrashing was the biggest time of the year?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, they'd try it one of the year. Yeah, well, thrashing. Yeah, that was about town harvest time. That was the biggest time. And we'd always have to get up about three or four o 'clock in the morning to get the things ready to get for breakfast, you know. And of course, they never were at the place anymore in a week, you know, or something like that. And of course, we had our own curried meats and everything, and they'd get some meat at the butcher shop. And Dad always had a keg of beer for the thrash years. He'd always haul that out.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: For each, for daily use, or for when they got through?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: We'd have no for daily use all the time. He'd have it for them, you know. He wanted to treat them, and he had a nice orchard out there. We had all kinds of fruit in there, too. He planted a little orchard, and we had everything out there. I, uh, just too bad I couldn't stay on the ranch no longer, and I loved the hills, but I, I couldn't stand it no more. I had to quit. You can stand hard and work just so long, then you gotta quit when it's time. All of the girls are going to school, going away while I'm married and gone. I'd seen one once a year. I had no other use, so I left the ranch and come in here.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I have lived here now about twenty - Is the ranch still, are the ranch buildings and all still there?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I sold it to Herman's. I had to give it up, but Dad homesteaded and everything. I have the original deed, number 213.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Hmm.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Is that number 213 for the state of Idaho, or what?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: For the county. For the county. Yeah.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Original North Idaho, I guess. There was many people, I can remember just the people had small houses and they all had big families, and they got along some way, and they could make a living on forty acres. They'd all go out and help one another, and things were cheap, you know, and you could find the women all sewed and they knit and knew how to make their clothes, but nowadays, of course, a lot of them can't sew around. They buy things where they're made, you know, and they're cold. Oh my, they cost a tremendous expense. The material is awful high.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No. Um, you said at one point that they used to grow a lot of flax here.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, that was the first crop. That's when they broke the land, that was all sowed, and it was too rich to grow wheat. The wheat used to be way high up, all grow stems and not too big ahead, so then they planted flax. That was the first crop, and that brought a good price.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And then I had a lot of - Soil?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, it was rich in soil, and then of course, then it finally come, why, they could raise wheat and barley and all things like that.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The soil was too poor for flax then, or what?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, there was more in wheat than other grains, and they needed that for their country, you see, to produce it. And there was a, let me see, there had been warehouses, and there was two warehouses burned, and a flour mill over here, and then we had two flour mills in Genesee. And up along the railroad track, why, I got a picture of Genesee, you can see how it used to look and what it looks like now. I took that picture.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, but those of you who can't look at it.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, I think I'll be able to see it.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: There was Genesee, in 1908.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Hmm.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Now there's flour mills, well I can't see. Okay. I can't, over there in Spokane, you had a gang of men who took, Sam and Joe, they were Austrians, he worked for them for three years, and so we used to take a double -under street car, and we'd go out there Sundays to the farmers, and he'd sidetrack it there on the side lane, and give Sam and Joe a work, you know, putting in little clinches around, and we'd pick us on the right -of -way fence and everything, and we'd go in there, and we'd have the best time, and then weekdays when they'd come in with the watermelons and rubber, and he'd just load the flat car with watermelons and take it up in the railway yards and tell the men to help themselves. And in the springtime, why he used to run the snowplows in the wintertime, in the springtime, why he used to, when the spring come, why the foreigners used to come to the door, you know, oh my, the garlic on, and they'd take their hat off and they'd buy a way down, and they'd ask for him, well they couldn't say his name in English very good, so he'd be sleeping for only snowplows, you know, in the wintertime, and they wanted to see him, they wanted a job. Well, they worked for him for two or three years, you know, so he used to have them. It's pretty hard on him to try and work around here, you know, when he come here, you know, and he couldn't get used to it, and he used to think the names are so funny around here, you know, that... Oh, here's old Felix Warren and his stage, that's the way he used to drive, see.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: He sat on stage, he'd sit on the co -op, and I'd think of it, you know, Mero...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you know Felix Warren yourself?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I barely remember him, that's all, I was little, see. Before five years old, you know, you can't remember so much, but still I was observing, and...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was he still driving the stage?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, yes, mhm, and he used to drive the stage up and down, back and forth, and stay there at the hotel when the folks had it. He was a smart old fella. And then here's closing buttons and bows, you know, that's the store that Watson's closed down there in... It's probably when they died, you know, or before they died, it says, High button shoes, gold placer pans, kerosene lamps, cure, all remedies, and numerous other items dated back in the early 1900s, will be sold here Tuesday and Wednesday during the closure of the Watson's General Merchandise Store. Well, when they speak up to 1900, you know, that don't seem long ago to me, because before 1900, why, I was around here observing everything, you know. So they sold out there, and they both moved to Lewes, and they died. They had their buggy whips, and... They had old buggy iron scales, and storage bins, and nail kegs, and buggy whips, and other items, maybe retained for their historical value. Maybe somebody got them, I don't know.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I'll tell you old Felix Warren, he was quite a friendly old guy.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did your grandfather, Tony, ever tell you about when he was working in the Erie Canal, or when he was working as a Pony Express writer? Did he ever tell you stories about those days?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, he said it was awful rough riding, he had to give it up, it was too hard on his health, he had to go so fast, and all kinds of weather and everything, and he had to go on a fast slope, you know, that's why the stampers got the picture of the pony running, you see. And he had to go so fast, and it was too hard on him, he did that six months, and he couldn't stand it. He had to quit. And then he worked his way out, you see, and he had a little money on the land here, he was better off than lots of them, you know. And most of them had to work their way through it. Oh, there's something funny, you know, he used to tell about, he had a neighbor, you know, and whenever he'd chew his tobacco, you know, he'd throw the cut out or throw it away, and the old fellow told him to save him for it because he's smoking his pipe. Well, they couldn't afford to buy tobacco, you know. No, that was it. And a lot later people come here to buy, investigate and investigate and buy land, and they'd always buy an equity, they'd have a chance to buy an equity out, you know. Get that way.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: To buy what?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Equity. That's a, you know what that is. And so, there's an old fellow below us there, oh, I was scared to death of him. The people that had it first, old Joe Brunte, he was bloated, he was farmed, at first he joined my father, he's the one that got my father to come there, you know, to this place. And my dad met him in Uniontown, near Uniontown, and he came up there and dad filed on the homestead. So, Joe Brunte was a good neighbor, but he went up to the mountains too, he had a little sawmill near Helmer, Idaho, up here. And he had a sawmill up there, and he got up in the morning and he went to light his pipe and the mill blew up because the pipes were frozen. Blew him all to pieces, he's buried out east of Genesee. So then there was an old fellow by the name of Clark, and he bought the equity out. And he lost it. The government put him out in the state, put him out on the county road, and us children used to look down and mother used to tell us to stay away from their user issues. And we were so scared of him, you know, and he was mean. And anybody's cattle or anything, we got along with him, but some neighbor had some cattle and he shot them full of buckshot and he was mean, you know, and all that and so forth.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was he mean to people too?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, he was an honor to get along with. So him and my father didn't hook too good and they wouldn't join fences, so they had a devil's lane between them. You know what that is, devil's lane. So my dad went to have his own, there's other people about the place, so I thought to myself, now I'm going down there, now I'm going to walk on the devil's lane, so I can say I walked on the devil's lane, so there's two rows of fence. So the other people that bought the equity from the government rebought it again, why they tore the fence down, there was one fence between dad and the neighbor. Oh, I tell you, there's an old clerk used to be afraid of places, old Joe Brunner used to be haunting all the time, you see him at night, you know. He's a ghost, you see. They're all superstitious. So that was it, but mother got along with Joe Brunner, she got along with poor old Clark too, you know, old Clark was vicious, but I guess he was just picked to death by people, you know, and that makes people mean sometimes too.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were there a lot of sort of eccentric or oddball kind of characters who ended up out here in the early days?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, some of them, people had to get, they called them nicknames, you know, they had to get along with them and everything, there was law and everybody tried to be congenial, you know, there was lots of cranky people, unreasonable people, you know, but you'll find that no matter where you go. But they all got along all right.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: But nobody in particular that you can think of, though?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, I don't know. One time there was a man murdered in town here, back of the, he was robbed and murdered back at the store here. In the early days my dad told me who it was and what it was over to, but I don't know, I forgot what he told me about that. My dad was 87 when he died. He was a cripple, he used to sit and talk. Take him to have the memory to tell the things. Well, he worked hard in his days too, so did old grandfather Tierney.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Grandfather Tierney was mostly a farmer? Oh yes, that was his work, he'd say, hey, homestead, did you come here in 1870?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: That was his idea, you know, he settled down on this land here and they always had to file him loose and there was no county courthouse. In Moscow they had to all go to the Nez Perce County Courthouse. And then in 1890, why, they just become a state and they divide the counties up to, I mean, later on, the Nez Perce County name, you see.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: So, that was it.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, poor old Father Cattell, he was a very grand man and Reverend Henry Spalding too. Oh, Mrs. Clyde and I, when we get together, we sure talk. I like to talk to her. Homer David was nice too, you know, I could talk to him an awfully lot too, you know. Oh, different people up there, so was a man, she was a sweet old person.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Guys that I ever knew, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The Gamble brothers?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh yes. You know, my dad loaned them money before he died and they paid me every cent back that was ever owed. And they were just nice to cook for. They harvested hay bailing up there at one of the neighbors. And so they came down about noon, just about noon, well, quarter after twelve, they got set up. Dark place, they just got through that place up to that quarter after twelve and they come down there and it was twelve thirty when they come down to our place, they tore down everything.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Boy, he went out and asked them if they had their dinner and they said no. And it was nearly after, it was about half past twelve. Well, I says, I can get dinner and I had everything ready in forty minutes, had a great big dinner on the table and it was the happiest lunch I ever cooked for. Awful nights, they were always very precise and nice, you know. Anytime I go anywhere and Gus and all of them, they always meet me, it's a hearty welcome, they're very nice people. Awful nights.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How many Gamble brothers were there?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, there was John and Burton and Gus. And then there was Lola and her sister that I knew of. But Lola, their father was one of the first ministers in Moscow, I guess. Grand old man, well he was an old man, but all the things that have changed so on here. I'm glad I live now, three decades now I might say, and I'm glad I live long enough to see the changes around here. Changes in the country, it don't seem possible.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Like what?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, everything is so progress, you know, changing things around and everything is so changed, you know. Now they're putting the wires on the telephone company underground and all this modernism and everything, well I just, well now I could go and live in a cabin and act and get along just like the pioneers did, I could, because I know what they did. And it's just one of the same modernism, appliances and stuff like this, you know. And I had a hard time getting used to electric range. I've got an old cook stove here yet and it's 44 years old. And I still cook on it. And then I got the electric range too. We cook on it when you're cooking. Yeah, and wintertime you can have fire in there and burn press to logs or put in there. My son all brings wood up for me and he, he just, and they're all good to me, you know, the children and I, they always bring things home and there's always something coming through the mail.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did the pioneers, what different ways did they preserve their food?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well they had a cellar and a cave in the hill.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And that was covered over with rails and straw on top and then dirt on top. And then in the front there was a space about that thick and that was all stuffed with straw and then they had double doors. And they had their potatoes and their rutabaggers and whatever they had in there. And grandfather, of course he had a wonderful basement and he always had a Dutch one to make his sauerkraut. And he had his sauerkraut in the basement there and he used to take mother down there and give her sauerkraut. He used to have plenty to eat, he always believed in having plenty to eat, always. And down in the old cabin, he never did move, after his second wife died he always lived down in the old log cabin, you know. So we went over there and my mother used to go, mother used to walk over from the ranch and put in there for them. She'd go over there and she'd drive over, ride her horse over and we'd go over there and there we'd step down into the kitchen and they had that little cook stove there. And she'd cook there and everything like that and we went over there one time and my uncle and aunt were there. And so my aunt used to can these huckleberries in these buckets and sarisberries.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What sort of buckets?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, these little lard pails.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How would you seal it?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Sealing wax. They made it some way, I don't know how. And so my cousin, he was just a little kid, he's passed on now. And he said to my younger sister, no my third older sister, he said, now give me a kiss, there was eating at the table. And he said, no, she says, your face is too dirty.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Little kids.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What other ways did they make their food last? What kind of food did they eat during the winter? What could they make last?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, they'd take the working butcher, we'd butcher five or six hog hogs and they'd make that sausage and there's dry cured meat. And then they'd take them hams and bacon.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How would they dry cure it?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Salt. Dry cured and some ate it Brian. And they'd throw it down there and then they'd hang it in the smokehouse and smoke it with any kind of wood they could get ahold of. That's the way they did it. And then that there, they'd put it back in this cave there where it was cold and they'd pack it in a straw, pack it up and go out in the summertime, get a ham. Somebody would come, grandfather always used to say, well, somebody come, oh now mama, go cook some bacon and eggs, go fry a couple eggs, go give them something to eat. He always thought about giving somebody some meat. So they cooked bacon and eggs and that tasted awful good when you take a team and drive a long ways. And then people used to gather around and have big dinners. And when Mike Evitts got married by his wife and him, they had the four daughters and he had a 12 room house. And that house was built just to love him. It's one of the finest houses in the country. And there wasn't a musical instrument invented but what he always got it. And he had the first organ.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Who is this?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Mike Evitts. E -V -I -T -T -S. That's Mabel Suter's grandfather. Mrs. Suter's grandfather. And that organ is over in Thorn Creek now in somebody's house. And he had the first organ up here on the hill. And he planned to run the first organ here. And that 12 room house is in good shape when they left there. And they left his son alone, big family living and everything. And they just sold it to somebody else and now the house is tore down. And there's nothing there now anymore. But my hospitality and they cooked meals. And the meals that they cooked, I never saw these Hungarian dishes. Something you never tasted before. And I asked my aunt one time, one of the daughters married my uncle. And she was a grand person. And I said one day I stayed over there for Easter. And they had something I never forgot. The ticket was ham and homemade bread and eggs and everything. And put together and they put it in a kind of container and they boil it. And then slice it off cold and it was delicious. So I asked Aunt Lucy one time how they made it. And she said, well they take a whole ham and a lot of bread and take 12 eggs. And we mix that all together. And then we put it in the boiler and then they sliced it off. And that was good. And the cakes and the salads they used to make, it was just wonderful. And they had a lot of sheep. And Grandma Evert used to take and shear these sheep. They'd shear the sheep, you know, and she'd card the wool, make yarn, and sew knit socks and sweaters and things. And then she'd go out to harvest time, she'd go out to the straw stack and she'd take sack twine and she'd go out and make a little basket, you know, weave them. A little handle in them. And she was handy that way. Oh, she could do just about anything. She's the one that always seated the grain by hand, you know. And they never went anywhere much taller. They couldn't write their name in English, you know, make a cross. But they could talk to you when you come there and they wore earrings.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Both of them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And they were Hungarians?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes. And they come from, he come there in 1870 and then he worked around for a couple of years and then he filed a complaint and then he sent it for his wife and his brother, half brother. And they had food there. I used to like to go over there and Annie and I, he was the youngest daughter, we used to play together, she's passed on too. And we used to have the best time but he had all good girls, though. They were real nice girls. They were awfully good to everybody. Always willing to give them a helping hand and Mike and Grandma, it always seemed to them that Mother always had plenty too. They'd try and help her if they could, you know. They thought a lot of her. She had so many friends, you know, things like that.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did your mother, was she having a hard time making it or something? You mean they took care of her after your father passed away?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: After her mother passed away. Until she went to Citrus School. They come over there then, see. They were awful good people.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did the early people out here do much work together?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did they cooperate, exchange work?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, so much of that. And that's how they got along so well, you see.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Like doing what kind of things?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, working on the farm and breaking horses and butchering and things like that, you know. Doing things that way. They used to have butchering bees, you know, they'd all gather one person's home and butcher hogs and cut them up, you know, and sold them down and everything. I learned how to do all that, you know. I could make sausage and everything, smoke it.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I learned how to do all those things, but I wish I had somebody who made sausage now. It was really good.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did they have barn raisings and things like that? Barn what? Barn raisings? Barn, you know, roof raisings or building bees or things?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, no. No, no, Dad paid for everything that he built. I had bills, you know. A man by the name of Bullwinkle built his house and his barn. And then they lived over the hill and these late miles. Old rustic cabin here had built why that had one big room that lined with cedar and then there was a little bedroom and a kitchen off of that. And that's where they lived, the homestead. Mother used to stay there all the time when she'd go to the mountains. She'd take care of all the stonking and pump water for them and everything. I don't know how she'd done it. She's smaller than I am. She could stand it though. She could tell you a story about pioneers and people of might. She had a memory. She sure did.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: How about, it seemed like you had a whole lot of different animals and pets and things when you were, as you were growing up as a woman. Are there any, you remember particularly, like any, I was thinking of horse teams.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did barbers kind of make pets out of the horse team sometimes?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, the horses. They had to be broke to work.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: My group of teams in Tennessee many a time rode horses too out of Buckskin, Cuyahou. Now there's my youngest daughter and her pet, Sheflin, pulling up there in the picture, you see. When I went back there, we had a Sheflin pony and my horse girl used to ride him to school before they had school buses. And then she had a white horse she used to ride. And she had to get out and walk on top of the snow drips and meet him in. That's where she got her first education. And then the younger girls, why the school buses started running then. And then of course I'd come in town and stay too with my dad, you know, when he was alone out there. Take care of him. I took care of him until he died. My sister would finally come and help me. I'd go back and forth, weekends, work in bachelor halls and do things like that.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Are there any of your horses or any of your animals that you remember in particular?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, there was Prince and Bill and Doc and Harry. And I could get on Harry, I'd be brought back and I could just ride him to look like everything. And everything, nothing about it, you know. But, cows was the main thing, you know. We used to make pets for the cows. I always liked cows. We used to milk cows and things like that. I'd milk many a cow. I always made lots of butter and had my customers. I miss all that, but there's a time you gotta give up, I guess. You can't do it all the time. Maybe other people have a chance. But they don't do that nowadays. I don't think some of the women could make butter even. I know I had a friend who had a store on the center of the avenue in Spokane and she took a sick. So, of course, I understood the grocery business too because I worked in the grocery store cashier a long time. So, when she was six, she sent for me and some of them young men come in and they'd have to buy bread. And they'd say, my wife can't even make a baked powder biscuit. Well, my goodness, I don't know what to say.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, that's it. I've been in the stores over here last time and some of these town women would say, oh, I had company Sunday night and I didn't have a loaf of bread and I couldn't do a thing. Well, I could turn out of, I used to love to cook and I could turn out of baked powder biscuits. No trouble, it's standing and talking. My older daughter said, even make dessert. She doesn't do it at all. I used to like to cook, but I can't rush around like I used to.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would you like to play the melodium again?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Is it always called melodium?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, just roller organ. Oh.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, well, you had another name you called it too. I forgot what it was.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Didn't you happen to have another name that you called it?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, it's Mrs. The people in Moscow call it melodium, but it's just hand organ. That's the name of it.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Just hand organ.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: That's what grandfather called it. It's called roller organ. All right. Well, I was glad to have it. I'm going to take that and put it back on the back end of the house because this door here, you know, there's so much mischief going on in town here, it's just terrible.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh,

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: grandfather, you just stand here and you just play Annie Laurie and you would stand over this and cry when you play Annie Laurie, you know.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: ...Translated?
